1: 'Hellenistic Jews' (Hellēnistai) vs. 'Hebraic Jews' (Hebraioi) — one of the most debated distinctions in Acts scholarship. The primary distinction is linguistic: Hellēnistai are Greek-speaking diaspora Jews (or diaspora-origin Jews resident in Jerusalem); Hebraioi are Aramaic/Hebrew-speaking Palestinian Jews. The secondary implications include: different liturgical traditions (LXX vs. Hebrew Bible), different cultural orientations (Greek diaspora vs. Palestinian Jewish), and different social networks. The Hellēnistai include the Seven elected in this chapter (all with Greek names: Stephen, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, Nicolaus).
2: 'It would not be right for us to neglect the ministry of the word of God in order to wait on tables' (ouk areston estin hēmas kataleipsantas ton logon tou theou diakonein trapezais) — the Twelve's distinction between diakonia logou (ministry of the word) and diakonia trapezōn (ministry of tables/meals) is not a hierarchy of spiritual vs. material but a division of labor within the community. Diakonia is the same root that produces 'deacon' (diakonos); the Seven are the antecedents of the diaconal office in Christian tradition, though the word 'deacon' does not appear in this passage.
3: 'Seven men from among you who are known to be full of the Spirit and wisdom' — the number seven has institutional precedent: the shivah tovei ha-ir (seven good men of the city) was the standard municipal leadership board in Jewish communities, responsible for civic and charitable administration (m. Megillah Acts 3:1; t. Megillah Acts 3:1; Josephus, Ant. 4.8.14). The Seven of Acts may be modeled on this institution, adapting a recognized form of communal governance to the new context. The qualifications ('full of Spirit and wisdom') combine pneumatological fitness with practical competence.
4: 'Give our attention to prayer and the ministry of the word' (tē proseukhē kai tē diakonia tou logou proskarterēsomen) — proskarterēsomen (we will devote ourselves to, we will persist in) is the continuous-attention verb: the Twelve will sustain an ongoing, undistracted practice of prayer and word-ministry. Luke uses the same verb for the disciples' continuous prayer before Pentecost (Acts 1:14) and the Jerusalem church's devotion to apostolic teaching and prayer (Acts 2:42). The Twelve's withdrawal from table-service is not an abdication of practical responsibility but a focused intensification of the prayer-and-word ministry that makes the word-ministry productive.
5: The election of seven men, all with Greek names, suggests either all-Hellenist appointees (addressing the Hellenist widows with leaders from their own community) or a deliberate cross-cultural selection. Nicolaus is specifically identified as 'a convert to Judaism from Antioch' (proselytos Antiocheus) — the only proselyte (former Gentile) among the Seven. This is the first appearance of Antioch in Acts, anticipating the Antioch mission of Acts 11:19-26. Stephen and Philip receive extended individual narratives; the others are not mentioned again.
6: 'They presented these men to the apostles, who prayed and laid their hands on them' — the laying on of hands (epitithēmi tas cheiras) with prayer is the commissioning/ordination gesture used throughout the OT (Numbers 27:18-23 — Moses commissions Joshua by laying hands on him) and NT (Acts 8:17; Acts 13:3; 1 Timothy 4:14; 2 Timothy 1:6). The gesture communicates transfer of authority, identification with the commissioned person, and invocation of divine blessing. Luke does not specify whether the whole community or just the Twelve laid hands on the Seven; both are grammatically possible from 'they presented them to the apostles, who prayed.'
7: 'The word of God spread. The number of disciples in Jerusalem increased rapidly, and a large number of priests became obedient to the faith' (polys te ochlos tōn hiereōn hypēkouon tē pistei) — the conversion of 'a large number of priests' is noteworthy: the priestly class was divided between Sadducees (who controlled the high-priestly offices) and ordinary priests (hiereis) who served rotational duty in the temple. The conversion of priests to the messianic movement created a community with substantial liturgical and theological expertise but disrupted the priestly establishment's institutional control. Josephus indicates that by the 60s CE the high priesthood was in severe tension with the ordinary priestly class (Ant. 20.8.8).
8: 'Stephen, a man full of God's grace and power, performed great wonders and signs among the people' — Stephen is the first of the Seven to receive extended narrative attention. His description ('full of grace and power') exceeds the original Seven's qualification ('full of Spirit and wisdom,' Acts 6:3) — Luke signals that Stephen is no ordinary table-server but an apostolic figure whose miracle-working ministry (the first attributed to anyone other than the Twelve in Acts) and theological confrontation with the Diaspora synagogues will drive the narrative toward Jerusalem's first martyr-death.
9: The 'Synagogue of the Freedmen' (synagōgē tōn Libertinōn) — Libertinoi (Latin libertini = freed slaves) are Jews whose ancestors had been enslaved and brought to Rome, later freed. Pompey brought Jewish captives to Rome in 63 BCE; many were subsequently freed and formed synagogue communities. The specific groupings (Cyrenians, Alexandrians, Cilicians, and Asians) suggest distinct diaspora community-synagogues in Jerusalem — a common arrangement where diaspora pilgrims and permanent residents maintained ethnically/regionally defined congregations. Cilicia is Paul's home region; some scholars have suggested Paul may have debated in this synagogue.
10: 'They could not stand up against the wisdom the Spirit gave him as he spoke' (ouk ischyon antistēnai tē sophia kai tō pneumati ō elalei) — the combination of wisdom (sophia) and Spirit (pneuma) echoes the Seven's original qualifications (Acts 6:3 — 'full of Spirit and wisdom') and fulfills Jesus's promise in Luke 21:15 ('I will give you words and wisdom that none of your adversaries will be able to resist or contradict'). The opponents' inability to refute Stephen's arguments corresponds to the pattern established with Peter and John (Acts 4:14 — 'there was nothing they could say').
11: 'We have heard Stephen speak blasphemous words against Moses and against God' — blasphemy (blasphēmia) against the divine name was a capital offense (Leviticus 24:15-16; m. Sanhedrin Acts 7:5). The charge that Stephen spoke against Moses (kata Mōyseōs) — the Torah-giver — and against God amplifies the charge: Stephen is accused of attacking both the human mediator of the covenant and the divine lawgiver himself. The false witnesses (Acts 6:13 — martyras pseudeis) parallel the false witnesses at Jesus's trial (Mark 14:55-59 — also charging temple-blasphemy), explicitly positioning Stephen's trial as a passion-narrative parallel.
12: 'They seized Stephen and brought him before the Sanhedrin' — the sequence (secret persuasion of false witnesses → stirring up the crowd, elders, and scribes → seizure → Sanhedrin appearance) follows the legal procedure for blasphemy cases outlined in the Mishnah (m. Sanhedrin Acts 7:5): two witnesses required, public hearing, formal Sanhedrin trial. The stirring up of 'the people and the elders and the teachers of the law' (ton laon kai tous presbyterous kai tous grammateis) is a deliberate crowd-mobilization that will produce not a legal verdict but a lynching (Acts 7:57-58).
13: 'This fellow never stops speaking against this holy place and against the law' — the charge against Stephen contains two components: speaking against the temple ('this holy place') and against the Torah ('the law'). Stephen's defense-speech (Acts 7) will address both: a sustained argument that the temple is not the necessary locus of divine presence (Acts 7:44-50) and that Israel has repeatedly failed to keep the Torah (Acts 7:2-53). The charge anticipates the content of what Stephen actually says — either the accusers are responding to genuine teachings of Stephen's, or Luke has structured the accusation to match the defense that follows.
14: 'This Jesus of Nazareth will destroy this place and change the customs Moses handed down to us' — the 'Jesus of Nazareth will destroy this place' accusation echoes Mark 14:58 / Matthew 26:61's false witness at Jesus's trial ('I will destroy this temple made with human hands and in three days will build another, not made with hands'). Jesus's actual temple-prediction (Mark 13:2 / Luke 21:6 — 'not one stone here will be left on another') was prophetic, not a personal threat. The 'changing the customs Moses handed down' (ta ethē ha paredōken hēmin Mōysēs) charge is the precursor to the ongoing Jewish charge against Christianity of Torah-antinomianism.
15: 'All who were sitting in the Sanhedrin looked intently at Stephen, and they saw that his face was like the face of an angel' — the angelic face-transfiguration is a theophany-sign in Jewish literature: Moses's face shone with divine glory after his Sinai encounters (Exodus 34:29-35); Daniel 10:6 describes the heavenly messenger's face 'like lightning'; the DSS (1QSa) includes a face like an angel as a qualification. The Sanhedrin's involuntary recognition of Stephen's angelic appearance provides the theological frame for his defense: the one whose face looks like an angel is not a blasphemer but a divine messenger. The visual sign prepares for the vision of the Son of Man at Acts 7:55-56.
